nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika; inhabits caves and crevices among rocks at depths up to ~125 m, though most commonly encountered shallower in the rocky coastal belt

decorPreferences: rocky caves and crevices for shelter and nocturnal retreats
sandy or fine-gravel substrate between rocks
dim lighting or shaded areas to accommodate nocturnal nature
secure tank lid (active swimmer at night)

diet.notes: Primarily carnivorous in practice — accepts frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, mysis, and other meaty invertebrate foods; high-quality sinking carnivore pellets and wafers also taken. Unlike many Synodontis species, significant vegetable matter is generally refused. Wild fish feed on algae scraped from rocks and small benthic invertebrates. Feed at lights-out to ensure adequate intake due to strictly nocturnal activity.

goodWith: medium to large Tanganyikan cichlids (Tropheus, Cyphotilapia, Altolamprologus)
other robust Lake Tanganyika fish of similar size
large rainbowfish in a Lake Tanganyika biotope
other Synodontis of comparable size (with adequate space and caves)
avoidWith: small fish that may be consumed at night (tetras, small rasboras, nano species)
timid or slow-moving bottom dwellers competing for the same caves

summary: Poll’s Synodontis is a heavily-spotted catfish endemic to Lake Tanganyika, growing to around 12–15 cm. It requires hard, alkaline water and a rocky cave setup, and is strictly nocturnal. Like others in family Mochokidae, it produces audible clicking sounds from its pectoral-fin spines. Captive spawning has not been recorded.
careNotes:
Poll’s Synodontis (Synodontis polli) is an endemic Mochokid catfish from the hard, alkaline waters of Lake Tanganyika, where it inhabits the rocky littoral zone. In the aquarium it requires the chemistry of its home lake: maintain pH 7.5–9.0, high general hardness (gH 8–20), and stable alkalinity; soft or acidic water is wholly unsuitable and will cause rapid decline. A temperature of 24–28°C is appropriate for the species.

A tank of at least 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is recommended to provide adequate territory and swimming space. Decor should replicate the rocky habitat: stacked flat stones, slate caves, and crevices are essential for security; this species tends to be secretive during the day and emerges primarily after lights-out. A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised, as Poll’s Synodontis can be active at the surface at night.

This species is strictly nocturnal and should be fed at lights-out to ensure it competes successfully for food. Meaty sinking foods — frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, mysis, and carnivore pellets — are readily accepted; unlike many related species in the same family, vegetable-based foods are largely refused. Do not leave uneaten food to foul the substrate — water quality must be maintained with efficient filtration and regular water changes, as elevated nitrates are generally poorly tolerated in a Lake Tanganyika setup.

Poll’s Synodontis produces audible stridulation sounds by rotating the base of its hardened pectoral-fin spine in its socket — a characteristic of the family Mochokidae, whose members are commonly known as squeaker catfishes. This sound is most often heard when the fish is startled or handled.

This species is sensitive to copper: avoid copper-based medications (often used to treat white spot disease) in any tank housing this catfish. Use copper-free alternative treatments if disease arises.

Poll's Synodontis tends to be semi-aggressive and territorial toward its own kind, but can be kept singly or in small groups if sufficient caves and visual breaks are provided. It is broadly considered compatible with robust Lake Tanganyika cichlids of comparable size. Small tankmates risk predation at night. This species is rarely available in the trade and is sometimes misidentified as the similar but smaller S. petricola; S. polli is distinguished by its larger adult size and irregularly shaped body spots.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning has not been recorded. In the wild, pairs form distinct breeding bonds before spawning, and the species is oviparous, depositing eggs rather than retaining them internally. Spawning in Lake Tanganyika is thought to coincide with seasonal flooding, which occurs approximately between July and October. No aquarium breeding protocol has been established, and spawning attempts have not been documented in hobbyist literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis polli
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (10 cm SL / up to 15 cm wild), temperature 75–82°F (24–28°C), pH 7.5–8.5, hardness 15–35°H, minimum tank 90 × 30 × 30 cm (80 L), diet preferences (meaty foods; vegetable matter largely refused), temperament, native range (Lake Tanganyika; possibly Lake Mweru), depth range (30–125 m), and breeding status (not recorded in captivity).

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis polli
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 14.8 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), native range (Lake Tanganyika, Africa), habitat (benthopelagic freshwater), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), trophic level 2.9 (omnivore), species named after Dr. Max Poll. Confirmed oviparous with distinct pairing during breeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Synodontis polli
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Provided habitat detail (rocky littoral, depths to 20 m), Lake Tanganyika water chemistry (pH 8.5–9, dH 4–15, temp 22–26°C), diet (algae scraped from rocks and small invertebrates), spawning season (July–October), coloration (olive-brown with large irregular black spots), and IUCN status (Least Concern). Also noted species described by Gosse 1982.

additional.name: FishBase — Mochokidae family page
additional.notes: Confirmed family classification (Mochokidae = squeakers / upside-down catfishes) and order (Siluriformes). Used to corroborate stridulation trait common to the family.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning has not been recorded. In the wild, fish form distinct pairs and deposit eggs, with spawning thought to coincide with seasonal flooding in Lake Tanganyika, occurring approximately between July and October. No aquarium breeding protocol has been established.
